questions.question: Explain the difference between static and dynamic arrays with examples.
keyPoints: Static arrays have fixed size at compile time, declared with fixed dimensions
Dynamic arrays allocate memory at runtime using malloc/calloc
Static arrays use stack memory, dynamic arrays use heap
Dynamic arrays allow flexible sizing but require manual memory management

questions.question: Define a structure to store student information (name, roll number, marks in 3 subjects). Write C code to declare an array of 10 such structures.
keyPoints: Define struct Student with name[50], rollno, marks[3]
Use typedef for convenience
Declare array: struct Student class[10];
Initialize using dot notation or initialization list

questions.question: What is a sparse matrix? How is it represented using arrays?
keyPoints: Sparse matrix has mostly zero elements
Store only non-zero elements to save memory
Use three arrays: row, column, value
Triplet representation: each non-zero element stored with its row index, column index, and value

questions.question: Explain the row-major and column-major ordering of 2D arrays with formulas.
keyPoints: Row-major: elements stored row by row (C default)
Column-major: elements stored column by column (Fortran)
Row-major address: base + (i*n + j)*size
Column-major address: base + (i + m*j)*size

questions.question: Write a C program to dynamically allocate a 1D array of n integers and find their sum.
keyPoints: Use malloc() to allocate: int *arr = (int*)malloc(n * sizeof(int))
Check for NULL pointer after allocation
Accept input and store in array
Calculate sum using loop, free memory after use

questions.question: What are unions in C? How do they differ from structures in terms of memory allocation?
keyPoints: Union stores all members in same memory location
Size of union = size of largest member
Only one member can hold value at a time
Useful for memory-efficient storage when only one field needed at a time

questions.question: Explain how polynomials can be represented using arrays. Give an example.
keyPoints: Array index represents exponent
Array value represents coefficient
For polynomial 5x³ + 3x + 2, array = {2, 3, 0, 5}
Zero coefficients still take space (dense representation)

questions.question: Write a C program to store and display information of n employees using an array of structures. Each employee has name, employee_id, salary, and department.
keyPoints: Define struct Employee with appropriate fields
Use malloc for dynamic array of structures
Input details using loop
Display using another loop
Free allocated memory properly

questions.question: Explain dynamic memory allocation functions (malloc, calloc, realloc, free) with examples. Discuss their differences and importance in array operations.
keyPoints: malloc: allocates uninitialized memory, single argument
calloc: allocates zero-initialized memory, two arguments
realloc: resizes previously allocated memory
free: releases memory back to heap
Proper use prevents memory leaks and dangling pointers

questions.question: Write a C program to multiply two matrices using 2D arrays. Discuss the time complexity of matrix multiplication.
keyPoints: Accept dimensions m×n and n×p
Use nested loops for multiplication: C[i][j] = sum(A[i][k] * B[k][j])
Time complexity: O(m×n×p)
Space: O(m×p) for result matrix

questions.question: Explain the concept of pointers and arrays in C. How are they related? How does pointer arithmetic work with arrays?
keyPoints: Array name is constant pointer to first element
arr[i] equivalent to *(arr + i)
Pointer arithmetic: adding 1 moves by sizeof(element) bytes
Pointers can traverse arrays efficiently
Difference between pointers and arrays: arrays cannot be reassigned, pointers can
